comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
PASS 1 plan review — T11 (Data-migration / backfill safety) [overlay]

This overlay applies only weakly. `compiled_state` is a derived projection re-built by the reducer from an append-only, file-per-ticket event log (`reduce_ticket`); SNAPSHOTs fold prior events and `process_snapshot` simply copies keys back into state (src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515-519). Adding `managed_refs` involves NO blocking DDL, NO large-table lock, NO giant transaction, and NO standalone backfill job — the field materializes per-ticket on the next reduce/compaction. So T11(a) online/expand-contract, (b) batching, and (e) blocking-lock-rollback concerns are largely not-applicable by construction (PASS).

AFFIRMATIONS:
- T11(a)/(b): No single blocking DDL on a large table and no one-giant-transaction backfill — the persisted format is a re-derived projection over per-ticket event files, so the new field appears incrementally without a migration job.
- T11(e): The append-only event log makes the change additive and non-destructive; a back-out (stop folding the field) cannot lose source events, so there is no data-loss-on-partial-failure risk.

FINDING (one transitional/backfill gap the plan under-specifies):

- [ ] Specify the behavior of `should_propagate_removal` for tickets whose LATEST SNAPSHOT predates `managed_refs` (the key is absent/empty until that ticket is next compacted). An empty `managed_refs` makes the gate ADOPT every inbound peer ref — so a legitimate LOCAL removal on a not-yet-recompacted ticket would be resurrected during the transitional window, which is exactly the churn this story exists to kill. The AC covers durability for a compaction performed under the NEW code, but not the EXISTING corpus of pre-change SNAPSHOTs. Add an AC for the absent/empty-`managed_refs` case and how it converges (e.g. re-derive the union from post-snapshot LINK/parent-EDIT events even when the SNAPSHOT lacks the key, or run/document a recompaction sweep).
  criteria: T11
  location: Acceptance Criteria — the `managed_refs` / compact_ticket bullets
  evidence: AC says "a removal performed before OR after a compaction boundary still propagates," but that test compacts under the new code; the plan does not state what happens for a ticket last compacted under OLD code, where `process_snapshot` restoring no `managed_refs` key is a silent no-op (src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515-519). This is the migration "pre-existing rows / dual-write window" dimension (T11-d) for a re-derived projection. The label precedent `local_label_intent` reads SNAPSHOT `compiled_state.tags` (src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/local_label_intent.py:63), so an absent-key fallback strategy is the relevant unknown here.
  suggested_fix: (empty — the convergence strategy, lazy re-derive vs. recompaction sweep, is a design choice for the author; either, once chosen and stated, satisfies the criterion.)

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body: PASS 2 independent verification (read-only). Confirmed against code: process_snapshot restores only present compiled_state keys (src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515-519), so legacy SNAPSHOTs lacking managed_refs leave it unset (findings 3/7/11 well-grounded; PASS 1's T11 finding corroborates). The shipped parent CLEAR fires whenever Jira carries any parent and local is None (outbound_differ.py:400-414), corroborating the Why framing that it can clobber a human-set Jira parent (finding 1/2 citation accurate). Consumer child tickets wake-inn-parse and the drum-ruler-suit lineage exist, confirming the scope deferral. The reclamation-prune vs monotonic-union tension and the prune/gate ordering (findings 4/6/9) are genuine unaddressed design risks. The kind set {parent,blocks,depends_on,relates_to} is a subset of CANONICAL_RELATIONS (docs/event-schema.md:51), relevant to finding 5's extensibility concern.

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — criterion T1 (prior-art / novel-architecture justification). This plan crosses two bright-lines (external-integration / provider-agnostic cross-system sync; a novel reusable provenance-set architecture), so T1 applies. Net: the prior-art instinct is strong (external field surveyed; `local_label_intent` cited as the failing in-repo analogue) but the single most-relevant body of prior art — this repo's own convergent-set / OR-Set / compaction-stability analysis — is not reconciled. Findings are coaching-level: clearable with citations, not rework.

AFFIRMATIONS (already pass T1):
- External prior art IS surveyed and named (Unito/Exalate/FolderSync/Syncthing/ghas-jira-integration), and the "Why" correctly extracts the load-bearing risks the field validates: state DURABILITY + unbounded growth. This is exactly the prior-art-before-committing move T1 asks for.
- Rule-of-Three / anti-repackaging is explicit: building the primitive ONCE is grounded in two named, real consumers (parent-hardening bug drum-ruler-suit/PR #54; link-removal bug wake-inn-parse, which I confirmed names this story as its parent and depends on the shared gate) plus future provider peers — not speculative abstraction.
- The novel mechanism is justified against the established-but-failing in-repo approach: it names `local_label_intent`'s event-history "ever-seen" projection and shows why it fails CLOSED across `compact_ticket`. Confirmed against code: `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515 process_snapshot` restores fields from `data.compiled_state`, and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/local_label_intent.py` projects from raw CREATE/EDIT/SNAPSHOT/TAG_DELTA events — so both the stated failure mode and the "maintain it in compiled_state so process_snapshot restores it for free" remedy are grounded.

FINDINGS:

- [ ] Reconcile `managed_refs` against the repo's own convergent-set prior art in `docs/remediation-implementation-plan.md` (the P2.3 tags / OR-Set analysis, esp. lines 102-127 and 679-741) before committing. That analysis already adjudicated this exact design class — a monotonic union of elements that must survive SNAPSHOT compaction — and rejected the OR-Set precisely for the two risks this plan calls load-bearing: unbounded tombstone growth and a "causal-stability requirement for safe compaction" (collapsing a monotonic set at a non-stable point silently resurrects a removed element or loses a concurrent add). `managed_refs` is a never-removed union folded into `compiled_state`; the plan should state why it does NOT inherit that causal-stability-vs-compaction hazard. [criteria: T1] [location: plan "Why" section ↔ docs/remediation-implementation-plan.md:102-127, 679-741] [evidence: plan cites external field + local_label_intent but never references the in-repo OR-Set/compaction-stability adjudication of the identical problem class; remediation-implementation-plan.md:115 "a causal-stability requirement for safe compaction (collapsing an OR-Set at a non-stable point silently loses a concurrent add or resurrects a removed element)"; plan AC "monotonic ... union ... never reduced by UNLINK/detach" + "survives compact_ticket"]

- [ ] Justify the novel "Bounded reclamation hook" (prune a managed_ref once absent BOTH locally AND in the peer snapshot) against the established alternatives the same prior art surveyed — version-vector "dots" (riak_dt_orswot) and causal-stability markers (Akka) — and argue why a converged-prune is a SAFE stability point here. The prior art (remediation-implementation-plan.md:112-117) flags convergence-style pruning as the exact place a removed element can be resurrected when re-merged after the prune; the plan asserts the prune stays bounded "without resurrecting human re-adds" but gives no argument for why peer-snapshot agreement is a sound causal-stability proxy under eventual/multi-provider delivery. This is the plan's genuinely novel element and most needs the anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three justification. [criteria: T1] [location: plan "What" → "Bounded reclamation hook" bullet] [evidence: docs/remediation-implementation-plan.md:112-117 (tombstone-growth + causal-stability hazards; version-vector dots vs tombstones); plan "prune ... once it is absent BOTH locally and in the peer snapshot (converged) ... without resurrecting human re-adds" — no causal-stability argument] [suggested_fix: Add a short subsection citing remediation-implementation-plan.md:112-120 that either (a) shows the single-peer Jira case makes peer-snapshot-absence a sound stability point (no concurrent in-flight add by construction) and flags that a SECOND provider re-opens the causal-stability question (reclamation re-justified per provider), or (b) defers reclamation until a stability detector exists, keeping managed_refs append-only for now.]

- [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion that the cited external tools use an "intent-gate" of the same shape this plan adopts. The "Why" states the field "validates the intent-gate concept" but cites five products as a block with no per-tool mechanism note; for the closest in-ecosystem analogue (ghas-jira-integration) confirm whether it gates removal on a PERSISTED provenance set vs. last-sync-state diffing — those imply different durability requirements and bear directly on the compaction-survival design. If unverifiable, downgrade the wording to a conceptual analogy rather than a capability claim. [criteria: T1] [location: plan "Why" section, prior-art sentence] [evidence: plan "The field (Unito/Exalate/FolderSync/Syncthing/ghas-jira-integration) validates the intent-gate concept" — five tools cited as a block, no per-tool mechanism; T1(c) requires unverified capability assertions be resolved.]

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — T11 (data-migration / backfill safety):

FINDING (T11, location: Acceptance Criteria — "managed_refs survives compact_ticket" / "What" §managed_refs): The plan adds a new persisted field to the SNAPSHOT `compiled_state` serialization (an overlay-triggering persisted-format change), and forward-replay reconstructs `managed_refs` for tickets with intact event history. But it is silent on ALREADY-COMPACTED legacy tickets: their pre-snapshot CREATE/LINK/parent-EDIT history is gone (folded into an existing SNAPSHOT that has NO `managed_refs` key), so on first replay `process_snapshot` restores a state with `managed_refs` absent/empty, and refs predating that snapshot can never be folded back in. Effect: for legacy tickets a current ref is NOT in `managed_refs`, so `should_propagate_removal` returns False and a deliberate local removal is silently NOT propagated — the exact churn this story exists to fix, now failing OPEN for the installed base. The plan should specify the backfill/seed behavior (e.g. seed `managed_refs` from the existing snapshot's current parent+deps on first reduce, and/or document that legacy tickets adopt-rather-than-remove until reconverged) and a back-out path.

- [ ] Specify how already-compacted legacy tickets (whose pre-SNAPSHOT history is gone and whose existing SNAPSHOT lacks `managed_refs`) acquire a `managed_refs` set — e.g. seed from the current snapshot's parent+deps on first reduce — so a deliberate local removal still propagates; and state the back-out behavior on partial rollout.

AFFIRMATIONS (T11): No blocking single-shot DDL / large-table lock — the change is to a derived, replay-reconstructed projection, not a separate persisted store, so no online/expand-contract DDL is required (criterion a satisfied). No giant backfill transaction — compiled_state is rematerialized per-ticket on replay/compaction, inherently incremental (criterion b satisfied). Resumability/idempotence is inherent: replay/compaction is re-runnable and deterministic, and the field's serialization is sorted for byte-parity (criterion c satisfied). The existing byte-parity guard is explicitly preserved (AC line), bounding format-change risk.

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
PASS 1 (T3 — Integration feasibility, overlay): PASS not-applicable. No findings.

Rationale: This story integrates only with internal, already-used machinery — the reducer (`process_snapshot`/`compiled_state`, present in `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515`), `compact_ticket` (`src/rebar/mcp_server.py:822`; commands at `src/rebar/_commands/compact.py`), and the outbound differ's existing intent-gate pattern that the plan explicitly mirrors (`local_label_intent`, `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_differ.py:1365` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/local_label_intent.py`). No external API/CLI/service/library is called or newly asserted in THIS story: the shared `should_propagate_removal` gate is explicitly provider-agnostic (operates on local logical refs), and the actual Linear/GitHub providers are explicitly OUT OF SCOPE per the plan's Scope section. Per the T3 overlay's own ANTI-FP scoping ("an internal, already-used integration is not-applicable"), the overlay does not apply: there are no external subcommands/endpoints to verify (MATCH/MISMATCH/UNVERIFIED), no auth/HTTPS preconditions, and no asserted-but-unverified external capability the story depends on.

Affirmations (T3): the intent-gate concept the plan reuses is already shipped and grounded in-tree (`local_label_intent` + `outbound_differ.py`), and the compaction-survival mechanism it relies on (`process_snapshot` restoring `compiled_state`) exists — so the capabilities this story asserts are internally feasible as described.

The plan names the in-repo analogue (`local_label_intent`'s "ever-seen" set) AND external prior art (Unito/Exalate/FolderSync/Syncthing/ghas-jira-integration), then explains precisely WHY the naive repackaging fails: `local_label_intent` projects from raw event history, which `compact_ticket` collapses to a SNAPSHOT, re-resurrecting post-boundary removals. - [T1b] The novel element (moving provenance from a history-scan projection into a reducer-maintained `compiled_state` field) is justified vs the established approach with a concrete failure mode (compaction durability), and is framed as a Rule-of-Three / anti-repackaging primitive ("build the durable primitive ONCE, shared by parent + links + future peers").

Naming this shows `managed_refs` is an ESTABLISHED reducer pattern (compaction-folded compiled_state map), which is a stronger anti-repackaging position than the external-tool citations alone — and pre-empts any "is this a novel architecture?" objection in later passes.
  - criteria: [T1]

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
PASS 1 review — T11 (Data-migration / backfill safety) [overlay]: PASS, no findings.

Affirmations:
- T11 (online / expand-contract): The "Migration" is a lazy, per-ticket reducer SEED computed at reduce/restore time from current deps + parent_id ("On the FIRST reduce/restore where `managed_refs` is absent ... the reducer SEEDS it from the ticket's CURRENT deps + parent_id"). This is a derived field reconstructed from the event log on every read — not a blocking DDL on a large table — so the no-downtime / expand-contract concern is satisfied by construction. Confirmed against src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515 (process_snapshot restores compiled_state field-by-field on every reduce).
- T11 (batching/scale): Per-ticket, recomputed on read; no giant single transaction to batch. The plan also notes per-ticket ref cardinality is small (tens). Not-applicable by construction.
- T11 (resumability/idempotency): Seeding is inherently idempotent and resumable — deterministically recomputed on every reduce; a partially-completed run simply re-derives on the next read. No double-apply window.
- T11 (dual-write window): No cutover window exists — the new field/event is "preserved-and-ignored by older clones (forward-compatible rollout; fsck WARNs on newer-than-binary state)." Writes during rollout are not lost because state is re-derived each reduce.
- T11 (rollback / data-loss): Explicit back-out posture documented — "disabling the fold leaves the gate a safe no-op" and "Missing/absent managed_refs defaults to EMPTY => the gate returns False (degrade to additive-only: no remove, no clobber, no churn-delete)." Partial failure degrades to additive-only with no data loss.

Rationale for PASS: T11 is an overlay for persisted-format DDL / large-table backfills requiring online/batched/cutover execution. This change is a non-persisted, lazily-derived reducer field (ANTI-FP: not-applicable for in-memory changes), and the plan's own text affirmatively covers the residual rollout/back-out concerns. No T11 findings.

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
[PASS 1 — G6 Approach soundness, mechanism & alternative-selection] Coaching findings + affirmations. All correctness reasoning grounded in the live reducer/differ code.

AFFIRMATIONS (criteria this chunk passes):
- G6 fitness-for-purpose: solves the NAMED problem, not a proxy. The "Why" precisely diagnoses why the raw-history "ever-seen" approach (the `local_label_intent` pattern) fails CLOSED across compaction, and the fix targets exactly that: `managed_refs` lives in `compiled_state`, which `src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:process_snapshot` (lines 515-519) restores by blindly copying EVERY compiled_state key. So "survives compact_ticket for free" is mechanically TRUE against the real code.
- G6 approach selection: I generated two structurally-different alternatives — (a) recompute the ever-seen set on-demand by scanning raw event history (the `local_label_intent` shape at `outbound_differ.py:1365-1372`), and (b) a sidecar provenance file/projection OUTSIDE compiled_state. (a) is the very approach the plan rejects with a grounded compaction-survival argument; (b) is strictly worse on codebase-alignment + blast-radius because folding into compiled_state rides the EXISTING process_snapshot/compaction machinery (verified) and the existing reducer-cache/byte-parity path. Chosen approach is defensibly at-least-as-good on every axis — PASS; alternatives discarded (not written into the plan).
- G6 mechanism (concurrency/atomicity): the fold + migration-seeding run inside the deterministic reducer replay (no concurrency at reduce time), so the "seed when absent" check-then-seed is NOT a TOCTOU. Strict reducer-side monotonicity (never pruned by UNLINK; `process_unlink` at :243-246 only shrinks `deps`) correctly keeps the resurrection window closed; deferring reclamation to a reconcile-time prune event avoids the prune-vs-fold hazard. Sound.
- G6 mechanism (empty/back-out): gate defaults missing `managed_refs` -> empty -> returns False (additive-only: no remove/clobber/churn), which doubles as a safe no-op back-out. Well-specified fail-safe.
- G6 positive rationale: PRESENT and strong (compaction-survival, single provider-agnostic primitive, fail-safe default, explicit back-out posture). No rationale finding.

FINDING 1 (G6 mechanism — peer-ref → local-logical-kind normalization for the gate lookup is under-specified; the direction-collapse case is load-bearing):
- [ ] Specify HOW the gate's CONSUMER normalizes a PEER (Jira) ref to the direction-distinguished local logical `(kind, target)` before the `managed_refs` lookup, because the Jira "Blocks" type collapses BOTH `blocks` and `depends_on` onto one link type, disambiguated only by side: `inbound_differ.py:498-502` maps the inward side of a Blocks link to `depends_on` and the outward side to `blocks`, and outbound dedup (`outbound_differ.py:995-998`) adds BOTH sides keyed by `(type_name, key)`. `managed_refs` is keyed on direction-distinguished kinds {parent,blocks,depends_on,relates_to}. The plan says the gate "operates on local logical refs", which is the right boundary — but it does not state that the consumer must first resolve the collapsed peer link to the correct logical kind/direction, so a `depends_on` ref managed locally could be looked up as `blocks` (or vice-versa) and the gate would wrongly deny (re-resurrect) or wrongly approve a removal. (Surfaced by the link-child wake-inn-parse's own G4 note as a within-child gap; it actually belongs to the PARENT gate contract.)
  criteria: [G6]
  location: plan "What" / gate definition "should_propagate_removal(ref, managed_refs) ... operates on local logical refs"; AC line "managed_refs ... normalized (kind, target)"; inbound_differ.py:498-502, outbound_differ.py:995-998
  evidence: "inbound maps Blocks inward->depends_on, outward->blocks (line 502) — the same Jira type yields two direction-distinguished local kinds"; "managed_refs keys on (kind,target) with kind in {parent,blocks,depends_on,relates_to} — a direction-collapsed peer link has no unique key without a normalization rule the plan omits"
  suggested_fix: State in the gate contract that the consumer passes an ALREADY-normalized local logical ref (kind+direction resolved via the same inward/outward rule as inbound_differ) and add a gate unit test covering a Blocks link whose inward/outward side maps to depends_on vs blocks, asserting the correct managed_refs membership decision for each.

FINDING 2 (G6 mechanism — parent-EDIT fold must capture the DISPLACED prior parent; no dedicated parent processor exists):
- [ ] Specify that the parent-EDIT fold reads `state["parent_id"]` BEFORE the EDIT merge overwrites it, and folds BOTH old and new parents into `managed_refs`. There is no dedicated parent processor — parent changes flow through the generic last-writer-wins `process_edit` (`src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:319-338`), which REPLACES `state[field_name]` (line 338) and never sees the prior value post-merge. A monotonic union of "every parent ever managed" therefore requires capturing the displaced parent pre-merge; folding only the post-merge value silently drops the prior parent and re-opens the resurrection window for RE-PARENTED tickets. The AC asserts the monotonic property but does not name this ordering dependency.
  criteria: [G6]
  location: AC line "Reducer maintains compiled_state.managed_refs ... folded from CREATE/LINK/parent-EDIT (including inbound-adopted), never reduced by UNLINK/detach" / src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:319-338 (process_edit), :56 (process_create parent_id)
  evidence: "process_edit is last-writer-wins with no dedicated parent processor — the displaced parent_id is unrecoverable once `state[field_name] = new_value` runs (line 338)"; "plan folds 'from parent-EDIT' but does not specify capturing the prior parent_id pre-merge, which is load-bearing for the stated strictly-monotonic union"
  suggested_fix: In the parent-EDIT fold, read the current state["parent_id"] (old parent) BEFORE applying the merge and fold BOTH old and new (kind=parent) targets; add a unit test that re-parents A->B and asserts managed_refs contains both (parent,A) and (parent,B), and that a later detach of B still propagates.

comments.author: Iso Tester
comments.body:
PASS 1 / T1 (Prior-art / novel-architecture justification): PASS — no findings.

The bright-line fires (novel architectural pattern + a migration/seeding step), and the plan clears T1 on all three checks:

(a) Prior art is CONSIDERED, not ignored. The plan explicitly names the in-tree precedent — the `local_label_intent` "ever-seen" set projected from raw event history (verified at src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/local_label_intent.py and its consumption in outbound_differ._diff_labels) — and diagnoses its concrete failure mode: `compact_ticket` collapses the event log to a SNAPSHOT whose compiled_state holds only CURRENT refs, so the event-history projection fails closed. External field prior art (Unito/Exalate/FolderSync/Syncthing/ghas-jira-integration) is cited as validating the intent-gate concept.

(b) The novelty is JUSTIFIED vs the established approach (anti-repackaging / Rule-of-Three). Rather than re-using the label-intent event-scan pattern, the plan moves provenance into a reducer-maintained `compiled_state.managed_refs` set precisely because that survives compaction "for free" via process_snapshot — verified at src/rebar/reducer/_processors.py:515-519, where process_snapshot blindly restores every compiled_state key. The Rule-of-Three is satisfied: the primitive is built once and consumed by parent (tan-elbow-mica) + links (wake-inn-parse) + documented future peers, not a one-off repackage.

(c) Capability assertions are resolved against the code: the load-bearing "compiled_state restored across compaction" claim is confirmed by process_snapshot's behavior; the migration is a lazy in-memory reseed on first absence (no DDL), consistent with the event-sourced model.

Affirmations: relevant prior art considered before committing; novel pattern's novelty justified vs the established (event-history-scan) approach; load-bearing capability assertion (compaction-survival via process_snapshot) verified.

(a) is the very approach the plan rejects with a grounded compaction-survival argument (compact.py:67-86 deletes the originals inside the lock, so any pre-snapshot removal is lost on re-derive). (b) is strictly worse on codebase-alignment + blast-radius (forfeits the existing process_snapshot/compaction/byte-parity machinery and adds reducer I/O the reducer avoids). Strict reducer-side monotonicity (never pruned by UNLINK; process_unlink at :243-246 only shrinks deps) correctly keeps the resurrection window closed; deferring reclamation to a reconcile-time prune event avoids the prune-vs-fold hazard. FINDING (G6 mechanism — the migration-seeding hook's PLACEMENT and ORDERING in the reduce path is under-specified, and it is load-bearing):
- [ ] Specify WHERE in the reduce path managed_refs seeding runs and that it executes as a post-replay finalization step (after the SNAPSHOT restore AND after all post-snapshot CREATE/LINK/parent-EDIT folds), not inside process_snapshot. process_snapshot (_processors.py:515-519) blindly copies compiled_state keys and would NOT seed an absent key; a pre-feature SNAPSHOT simply lacks managed_refs. ---

### Per-criterion verdicts

**AC1 — Reducer maintains `compiled_state.managed_refs` as strictly-monotonic, deterministically-sorted union…** ✅ MET

`_managed_refs.py` exports `add_managed_ref` (idempotent, monotonic, never removes). `process_create` calls it for the initial `parent_id` (`_processors.py:60`); `process_link` calls it for `(relation, resolved_target)` (`_processors.py:248`); `process_edit` calls it when `field_name == "parent_id"` (`_processors.py:351-352`). `process_unlink` only filters `state["deps"]` — never touches `managed_refs` (`_processors.py:251-254`). Serialized as `sorted(refs)` by `serialize_managed_refs`. `make_initial_state()` seeds the field as `[]` so the `process_edit` guard (`if field_name not in state: continue`) never silently drops it.

**AC2 — Reducer seeds `managed_refs` from current deps + parent_id when absent; test proves pre-feature compacted ticket's unlink propagates** ✅ MET (seeding confirmed; propagation chain covered by separate unit tests)

`process_snapshot` at `_processors.py:542-543` seeds from `seed_managed_refs_from_current(state)` when `"managed_refs" not in compiled_state`. `test_snapshot_without_managed_refs_is_seeded_from_current` (test file line 92-112) writes a SNAPSHOT with no `managed_refs` key (simulating a pre-feature compacted ticket), reduces it, and asserts the legacy parent and dep refs appear in `managed_refs`. The propagation half is covered by the gate unit tests (`test_gate_propagates_only_managed_refs`) — a seeded ref in `managed_refs` causes `should_propagate_removal` to return True, which the differ acts on.

**AC3 — `managed_refs` survives `compact_ticket` (restored by `process_snapshot`): removal before OR after compaction still propagates; covered by a compact-then-reconcile test** ❌ NOT MET

`test_managed_refs_survive_compaction_roundtrip` (test file lines 129-148) verifies that refs survive the SNAPSHOT roundtrip at the **reducer** level: it writes events, reduces, writes the compiled_state as a SNAPSHOT, reduces again, and asserts the refs are unchanged. This covers the "compaction survival" half. However, the AC explicitly calls for "a compact-then-reconcile test" — i.e., a test that compacts, then writes a post-compaction UNLINK (or exercises the outbound differ with the compacted ticket), and verifies the removal propagates through the reconciler. No such end-to-end compact→unlink→reconcile test exists anywhere in the test tree. The two halves are tested in isolation (reducer-only compaction roundtrip + separate differ-only gate tests), never combined into the single "compact-then-reconcile" test the AC specifies.

**AC4 — SNAPSHOT byte-parity preserved (existing byte-parity guard passes)** ✅ MET

`tests/interfaces/store/test_canonical_event_bytes.py` exercises the full lifecycle including `compact-on-close` which writes a real SNAPSHOT from `compiled_state`. `managed_refs` is included in `make_initial_state()` and serialized via `serialize_managed_refs` (deterministic sorted list), so every SNAPSHOT that carries `managed_refs` is byte-stable.

**AC5 — `should_propagate_removal` gate exists, provider-agnostic, defaults missing managed_refs to empty, unit-tested for REMOVE-vs-ADOPT** ✅ MET

`_managed_refs.py:139-152` implements `should_propagate_removal(kind, target, local_ticket) -> bool`. No Jira-specific logic (operates on local (kind, target) pairs). `test_gate_propagates_only_managed_refs` and `test_gate_defaults_missing_managed_refs_to_no_removal` cover REMOVE vs ADOPT decisions and empty/missing defaults. Both parent and link paths import and call it (`outbound_differ.py:518,1126`).

**AC6 — Operator-facing regression: unlink a managed ref locally, run two reconcile passes, the ref stays removed (not resurrected)** ❌ NOT MET

`test_outbound_remove_suppresses_inbound_link_readd` (`test_link_removal_sync.py:147-169`) covers the **single-pass, same-pass coordination** scenario: outbound emits REMOVE, inbound with that context suppresses the re-add. This demonstrates the churn is fixed within one pass.

The AC requires a **two-pass** regression test: pass 1 sends the DELETE to Jira; pass 2 runs with an updated Jira snapshot (the link is now absent) and must emit zero mutations — the ref does **not** resurrect. No such test exists. The closest tests are `test_managed_link_absent_locally_emits_remove` (pass-1 outbound remove), `test_outbound_remove_suppresses_inbound_link_readd` (same-pass inbound suppression), and `test_link_still_present_locally_emits_no_remove` (no-remove when local dep still present), but none simulates pass-2 state (Jira snapshot with link absent after deletion) and verifies zero mutations.

**AC7 — `docs/` documents the pattern (gate contract, `managed_refs` field, future reconcile-time reclamation hook)** ✅ MET

`docs/managed-refs.md` documents: the `managed_refs` field and its semantics (lines 38-87); the `should_propagate_removal` gate contract with its signature and True/False semantics (lines 91-104); the future reclamation hook as an explicit out-of-scope item (lines 113-121); the extension template for new providers (lines 123-127).

**Success Criterion — Reusable, provider-agnostic primitive both parent and link removal sync consume, with no Jira-specific logic in the shared gate** ✅ MET

`should_propagate_removal` operates on local `(kind, target)` pairs; all Jira-specific resolution (key → local_id) happens in the caller before the gate is invoked. Both `_is_parent_managed_removal` (`outbound_differ.py:510-526`) and `_diff_links_removal` (`outbound_differ.py:1120-1175`) consume it.

---

### Summary

Six of seven acceptance criteria and the success criterion are met. Two criteria fail:

1. **AC3** — The AC calls for a "compact-then-reconcile test." The `test_managed_refs_survive_compaction_roundtrip` test demonstrates compaction survival at the reducer level only; no test chains compaction → post-compaction event → reconciler/differ path to verify removal propagates end-to-end across the compaction boundary.

2. **AC6** — The AC requires a two-pass operator regression. The existing test covers same-pass suppression. A second-pass stability test — where the Jira snapshot reflects the deletion from pass 1 and pass 2 produces zero mutations — does not exist.

**Verdict: FAIL**

**Success Criterion — Reusable, provider-agnostic primitive both parent and link removal sync consume, with no Jira-specific logic in the shared gate** ✅ MET

`should_propagate_removal` operates on local `(kind, target)` pairs only. Jira-specific resolution (Jira key → local id) is performed by the caller before invoking the gate. Both consumers:
- `_parent_clear_is_managed` (`outbound_differ.py:507–526`): resolves the Jira parent key to a local id via `binding_store.get_local_id`, then calls `should_propagate_removal("parent", parent_local_id, ticket)`
- `_diff_link_removals` (`outbound_differ.py:1113–1126`): resolves Jira link targets to local ids, then calls `should_propagate_removal`

No Jira-specific logic in the shared gate itself.

---

### Note on prior FAIL verdicts

Two prior verification rounds returned FAIL, each on AC3 and/or AC6. The AC6 failure was already resolved in the second round (`test_two_passes_managed_unlink_stays_removed` at lines 264–306 is a genuine two-pass test with a mutable Jira). The AC3 failure was a stale-state issue: `test_unlink_after_compaction_still_propagates_removal` (lines 206–262) — which fully chains compaction → post-compaction UNLINK → outbound differ → asserts REMOVE — was absent in the file state those rounds read but is present in the current file.

---

**Verdict: PASS** — all seven Acceptance Criteria and the Success Criterion are met.

compiled_state.description:
## Why

Cross-system sync (Jira today; Linear / GitHub Issues planned) must propagate a LOCAL removal of a reference (detach a parent, unlink a dependency) WITHOUT it being resurrected by the next inbound pass. The naive approach — gate outbound DELETE on an 'ever-seen' set projected from raw event history (as `local_label_intent` does for labels) — fails CLOSED across `compact_ticket`: compaction collapses the event log to a SNAPSHOT whose `compiled_state` holds only CURRENT refs, so a removal performed at/after the compaction boundary is re-resurrected (the exact churn). We also found the shipped parent CLEAR (drum-ruler-suit / PR #54) is un-gated and clobbers a human-set-in-Jira parent on a locally-parentless ticket.

The field (Unito/Exalate/FolderSync/Syncthing/ghas-jira-integration) validates the intent-gate concept; the load-bearing risks are state DURABILITY and unbounded growth. This story builds the durable, reusable primitive ONCE so parent + links (and future peers) share one provider-agnostic pattern.

## What

A monotonic, compaction-surviving `managed_refs` provenance set maintained by the reducer in `compiled_state` (so `process_snapshot` restores it for free across compaction), plus a shared, provider-agnostic gate the outbound differ uses to decide REMOVE-vs-ADOPT for any reference kind.

- `managed_refs`: the union of every logical reference this ticket has ever managed — normalized `(kind, target_local_id)` where kind in {parent, blocks, depends_on, relates_to}. Folded in process_create (initial parent + deps), process_link, and parent EDIT (incl. inbound-adopted refs); STRICTLY MONOTONIC — never reduced by UNLINK/detach. Deterministically serialized (sorted list) for SNAPSHOT byte-parity.
- Shared gate `should_propagate_removal(ref, managed_refs) -> bool`: a peer ref present + locally absent => propagate DELETE iff ref in managed_refs; else ADOPT inbound. Provider-agnostic (operates on local logical refs; each provider maps to its entity). Missing/absent managed_refs defaults to EMPTY => the gate returns False (degrade to additive-only: no remove, no clobber, no churn-delete). This is also the back-out posture — disabling the fold leaves the gate a safe no-op.

## Migration (existing + pre-feature tickets)

On the FIRST reduce/restore where `managed_refs` is absent (old SNAPSHOT, pre-feature ticket), the reducer SEEDS it from the ticket's CURRENT deps + parent_id. Seeding is safe in both directions: a ref already in local deps was either created locally or inbound-ADOPTED (rebar owns it -> removable); a Jira-only ref never in local deps is not seeded -> still adopted, never clobbered. A new event type / field is preserved-and-ignored by older clones (forward-compatible rollout; fsck WARNs on newer-than-binary state).

## Reclamation (bounded growth)

managed_refs is strictly monotonic in the reducer (no prune there — pruning in the UNLINK path would contradict monotonicity and re-open the resurrection window). Per-ticket ref cardinality is small (tens), so unbounded growth is not a practical concern at this scale. A safe reclamation requires the PEER snapshot (a ref converged = absent locally AND absent on the peer) and therefore must be a reconcile-time step emitting an explicit prune event — that is documented as a FUTURE hook and is OUT OF SCOPE for v1 (deferring it avoids the prune-vs-fold concurrency hazard entirely).

## Scope

In scope: the reducer `managed_refs` field + compaction preservation + byte-parity + migration-seeding; the shared provider-agnostic gate helper (with its own unit tests); docs establishing the reusable pattern. The parent + link CONSUMER integrations are delivered by the child tickets (tan-elbow-mica, wake-inn-parse) which import this gate; THIS story verifies the gate's API + decision via unit tests, not cross-ticket call-sites. Out of scope: active reclamation/GC (future reconcile-time prune); actual Linear/GitHub providers.

## Success Criteria

- [ ] A reusable, provider-agnostic managed-ref provenance primitive both parent and link removal sync consume, with no Jira-specific logic in the shared gate.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Reducer maintains `compiled_state.managed_refs` as a strictly-monotonic, deterministically-sorted union of normalized (kind, target) refs, folded from CREATE/LINK/parent-EDIT (including inbound-adopted), never reduced by UNLINK/detach.
- [ ] When `managed_refs` is absent on reduce/restore (pre-feature / old SNAPSHOT), the reducer seeds it from current deps + parent_id; a test proves a pre-feature compacted ticket's existing refs become managed (its later unlink propagates).
- [ ] `managed_refs` survives `compact_ticket` (restored by process_snapshot): a removal performed before OR after a compaction boundary still propagates and does NOT resurrect — covered by a compact-then-reconcile test.
- [ ] SNAPSHOT byte-parity is preserved (deterministic serialization; the existing byte-parity guard passes).
- [ ] A shared `should_propagate_removal(ref, managed_refs)` gate exists, is provider-agnostic (no Jira-specific logic), defaults missing managed_refs to empty (returns False = no removal), and is unit-tested for the REMOVE-vs-ADOPT decision: a managed ref absent locally => propagate; a never-managed peer ref => adopt.
- [ ] An operator-facing regression proves the churn is observably fixed: unlink a managed ref locally, run two reconcile passes, the ref stays removed (not resurrected).
- [ ] docs/ documents the pattern (the gate contract + the managed_refs field + the future reconcile-time reclamation hook) as the template for future peer-sync providers.
compiled_state.title: Managed-reference provenance: a compaction-surviving, provider-agnostic removal-sync gate
